response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I need to be honest with you about this social media business. I am not on these platforms every day, maybe a few times during the week when I have a moment after work, and honestly, the fake news situation worries me deeply. As someone who fixes pipes for a living in Naples, I deal with real problems that have real solutions. You cannot fix a leak with lies, but online everything seems twisted and confusing. When I scroll through Facebook or whatever, I see things that make my heart race with anxiety. Is this health scare real? Is that political news true? I did not have much formal education, just primary school, so sometimes I feel like I cannot tell what is trustworthy anymore. The people writing these things use complicated language and official-looking images that seem designed to confuse people like me. What really bothers me is that I do not have time to check everything. I am self-employed, running my own plumbing business, and when I finally sit down in the evening, I want to relax, maybe read a novel or do some knitting, not become some kind of detective investigating every post. But then I worry I will share something false with my daughter or my partner, and that anxiety just builds up. My approach, and I will tell you straight because I do not sugarcoat things, is to mostly ignore what seems too dramatic or sensational. If something makes me feel panicked or angry immediately, I have learned to just keep scrolling. I also try to remember my faith teaches me to seek truth and not spread falsehoods, so that helps guide me. When something seems important, I might ask my daughter about it since younger people seem better at spotting these tricks. Otherwise, I stick to pages from actual newspapers or official sources, even if I do not always understand everything perfectly. The whole situation frustrates me because these platforms should do more to protect users instead of letting liars run wild, but since they will not fix their own pipes, so to speak, we have to protect ourselves as best we can.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 62-year-old self-employed female plumber in Naples with primary school education, anxious personality, low conscientiousness, low agreeableness, and low extraversion. She uses social media only a few times weekly. Her approach should reflect: 1) practical, working-class perspective from plumbing background, 2) anxiety about misinformation given her psychological feature and moderate neuroticism, 3) straightforward, somewhat blunt tone from low agreeableness, 4) acknowledgment of educational limitations creating vulnerability, 5) limited time/energy for fact-checking due to self-employment and low conscientiousness, 6) reference to Islamic values about truth, 7) reliance on simple heuristics rather than systematic verification, 8) frustration with platforms and situation, 9) practical coping strategies that fit her limited engagement. The tone should be direct, somewhat pessimistic, anxious, and practical rather than warm or highly analytical.
response_parsed.tone: direct, anxious, somewhat blunt, practical, frustrated
